FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (n = 4,641 ages 9-12) at baseline, Year1, and Year2 follow-up'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) measured specifically: 'time spent watching TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting on electronic devices, visiting social networking sites, and video-chatting'

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) testing bidirectional relationships between SMA and psychopathology outcomes. Multiple inferential tests reported linking SMA to sleep duration (β = -0.10, p = 0.004), externalizing symptoms (β = 0.07, p = 0.014), and internalizing symptoms (β = 0.09, p < 0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CIs reported: 'β = -0.10 [95% CI: -0.16, -0.03], p = 0.004' for SMA predicting sleep duration; 'β = 0.07 [95% CI: 0.01, 0.13], p = 0.014' for externalizing predicting SMA; mediation indirect effect = 0.032 [95% CI: 0.003, 0.067], p = 0.030

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal CLPM design with discovery and replication sets examining temporal relationships

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2024). Not a preprint, protocol, or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper reports SMA as OUTCOME but also reports reverse direction (sleep/externalizing predicting SMA). Moreover, paper includes individual psychological predictors (externalizing/internalizing symptoms are psychological/behavioral variables, not just demographics). Mediation analysis also included (structural brain covariation mediating SMA-externalizing link). Cross-sectional component in CLPM concurrent correlations also present.
notes: Bidirectional associations examined. Mediation pathway tested (structural brain covariation on SMA-externalizing relationship). Both discovery and replication samples.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'Participants were 10,828 youth enrolled in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study...Data were from ABCD Release 4.0 (September 2021)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured specifically via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey: 'youth self-reported their screen time via the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. This instrument asks participants to report their usual time spent (hours/day) on six different screen types (viewing/streaming television shows or movies, watching/streaming videos [e.g., YouTube], playing videogames, texting, videochatting, and onsocial networking sites) on weekdays and weekend days separately'

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression models: 'multilevel logistic regressions with the study site specified as a random effect were used to test the associations of interest' linking baseline screen time and sleep duration to follow-up internalizing symptoms. Multiple significant tests reported (e.g., OR 0.6, 95% CI 0.4-0.8; OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1-2.2)

FT_IC4.evidence: Extractable numeric values: adjusted odds ratios with 95% CIs reported: 'In females, longer baseline sleep duration was protective against withdrawn/depressed symptoms (odds ratio [OR] 0.6, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.4-0.8)...greater baseline weekend screen time was associated with increased risk of withdrawn/depressed symptoms (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1-2.2)'. Tables 3 and 4 contain extensive OR/CI values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal study design with one-year follow-up examining bidirectional associations between sleep duration, screen time, and internalizing symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024). Published manuscript, not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper reports BOTH screen time as independent variable (predicting internalizing symptoms) and also tests reverse direction (internalizing symptoms predicting screen time/sleep). Includes individual psychological predictor (internalizing symptoms themselves are behavioral/psychological outcomes). Longitudinal but includes bidirectional analyses and individual (not just demographic) predictors.
notes: Analyses stratified by gender. Examined bidirectionality explicitly. Screen time measured separately for weekdays and weekend days. Multilevel design accounting for site clustering.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'Data were baseline (2017-2018) and one-year follow-up (2018-2019) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, a multi-site study of a nationally representative sample of U.S. youth (N = 10,544)...We used data from ABCD Release 4.0 (released in September 2021)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured specifically in three categories: 'Youth self-reported their screen time via the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey...The forms of screen time measured were viewing/streaming television shows or movies, watching/streaming videos (e.g., YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting, and using social networking sites' collapsed into 'streaming' (viewing/streaming + YouTube), 'gaming' (video games), and 'socializing' (texting + video chatting + social networking)

FT_IC3.evidence: Isotemporal substitution linear regression and compositional data analysis (CoDA) models with inferential tests linking different screen time types to BMI z-score outcome. Multiple regression coefficients reported with statistical tests (p-values and confidence intervals)

FT_IC4.evidence: Extractable numeric values: β coefficients with 95% CIs reported: 'In females, isotemporal substitution models estimated that replacing 30 min of socializing (β [95% CI] = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.002]), streaming (-0.03 [-0.05, -0.01]), or gaming (-0.03 [-0.06, -0.01]) with 30 min of physical activity was associated with a lower follow-up BMI z-score'. In males: 'replacing 30 min of socializing (-0.03 [-0.05, -0.01]), streaming (-0.02 [-0.03, -0.01]), or gaming (-0.02 [-0.03, -0.01]) with 30 min of sleep was associated with a lower follow-up BMI z-score'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal study design (baseline 2017-2018 to one-year follow-up 2018-2019) using isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis to examine associations between screen time categories and BMI

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2024). Published manuscript, not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper examines screen time as INDEPENDENT variable predicting BMI (non-SM outcome). The directionality is screen time -> BMI (or time-displacement patterns), not screen time as dependent variable. Multiple non-demographic predictors considered (physical activity, sleep duration behaviors). This is a straightforward screen time as IV analysis.
notes: Sex-stratified analyses. Uses isotemporal substitution (30-min replacements) and compositional data analysis. Examines three distinct screen time categories separately (streaming, gaming, socializing). Complex survey design approach accounting for site clustering and sampling weights.
